title: COMIC: Agentic Sketch Comedy Generation
research_questions: Can a fully automated AI system generate short sketch-comedy videos that are funny to human viewers?
How can LLM-based humor critics be aligned with real viewer preferences without fine-tuning a dedicated critic model?
Does iterative pairwise competition among scripts, organized into multiple critic-governed islands, improve comedy script quality and diversity?
Does critic-guided iterative rendering improve long-form comedic video generation compared with single-pass agentic video pipelines and frontier text-to-video models?
tasks: storyboarding and visual realization for comedy sketches

datasets_used.name: YouTube sketch comedy channels corpus (Foil Arms & Hog, Key & Peele, SNL, Studio C, Viva La Dirt League)

methods_proposed.name: COMIC
methods_proposed.summary: A fully automated multi-agent sketch comedy video generation system that takes character images, voices, text descriptions, and background assets as input and produces 1–2 minute comedy scripts with corresponding video and audio. The pipeline uses agentic roles including writer, critic, editor, scene director, image/video generators, and voice generator.

methods_proposed.name: Generate-and-select viewer-aligned critics
methods_proposed.summary: The paper synthesizes diverse LLM critic personas with a meta-critic and selects critics whose pairwise preferences best match engagement tiers derived from normalized YouTube view counts. Task-specific critics are selected separately for Top vs. Middle and Top vs. Bottom comparisons.

methods_proposed.name: Island-based competitive script writing loop
methods_proposed.summary: Scripts are partitioned into multiple isolated islands, each governed by a different committee of aligned critics. Within each island, scripts undergo round-robin pairwise tournaments, and losing scripts are revised using critic feedback.

methods_proposed.name: Script-conditioned video rendering loop
methods_proposed.summary: For each selected script, a scene director creates storyboards and rendering critics iteratively refine shots. The system uses history-level single-elimination tournaments to choose the best shot revisions and a final scene-level tournament to choose among complete video realizations.

methods_proposed.name: Engagement normalization for critic alignment
methods_proposed.summary: The paper fits per-channel logistic growth models to YouTube view-count trajectories and uses projected carrying capacity as an engagement score for selecting top, middle, and bottom tiers of sketches.

evaluation_methods.detail: Blind randomized human evaluation of COMIC, VGoT, MovieAgent, Veo 3.1, and Sora 2 with 22 responses per method and 110 responses total; participants rated videos on 7-point Likert scales for funniness, watch-more intent, comparison to human comedy, script quality, narrative quality, realism, and consistency.
Paired A/B ablation comparing full COMIC against a critic-free baseline across funniness, script quality, narrative quality, visual realism, and visual consistency; neutral responses were excluded.
Aligned LLM critics perform pairwise comparisons between scripts or videos, including Single Best, Mean Critic, Task-Wise Best, and Channel-Wise Best aggregation strategies.
Win Rate, Inter-Diversity, and Intra-Diversity are computed from pairwise evaluator probabilities over generated samples versus references.
YouTube view counts are normalized by video age using per-channel logistic growth models and used as empirical audience engagement signals for critic alignment.

headline_results.finding: Task-Wise Best critic selection achieved the highest average validation accuracy for script engagement discrimination, beating Mean Critic and Single Best on both comparison tasks.
headline_results.value: 0.64 (Top vs. Middle average); 0.83 (Top vs. Bottom average)

headline_results.finding: Task-Wise Best critics also generalized best to held-out scripts.
In human evaluation, COMIC had the highest funniness score, above Sora 2, Veo 3.1, VGoT, and MovieAgent.
On comparison to human comedy, COMIC scored between Slightly Less Funny and Comparable, higher than all baselines.
In automated video evaluation against middle-tier test videos, COMIC had the highest win rate under both critic aggregation strategies.
Human raters preferred full COMIC over the critic-free ablation across all measured dimensions.
headline_results.metric: A/B preference for COMIC vs. No Critics
headline_results.value: 57% vs 43% Script; 71% vs 29% Narrative; 88% vs 12% Realism; 100% Consistency; 62% vs 38% Funniness

key_findings: Task-wise critic selection aligned to YouTube engagement tiers outperformed pooled and single-best critic baselines on both validation and held-out test comparisons.
COMIC outperformed the agentic video baselines VGoT and MovieAgent by large margins across all human-evaluated dimensions.
COMIC beat Sora 2 and Veo 3.1 on funniness, watch-more intent, comparison to human comedy, script quality, and narrative quality, while Sora 2 and Veo 3.1 scored higher on realism and consistency.
The automated video evaluation ranking, COMIC > Sora > Veo > MovieAgent approximately VGoT, aligned with the human evaluation results reported in Table 3.
The multi-island topology yielded higher overall win rate and intra-diversity than a single-island configuration.
The critic-free ablation was weaker across script, narrative, realism, consistency, and funniness, indicating that iterative multi-agent critic refinement is central to the system’s performance.
limitations: The iterative refinement process incurs computational costs, although the paper notes that local structures can be parallelized.
Normalized YouTube view counts are used as a proxy for humor quality, but this may introduce noise from clickbait and algorithmic promotion.
The paper identifies adding sound effects as future work to enrich the audio-visual experience beyond dialogue.
The paper identifies model-output attribution and originality quantification as future work when building on large internet corpora.
COMIC scored lower than Sora 2 and Veo 3.1 on human-rated realism and consistency, partly because COMIC’s longer output durations create more opportunities for visual artifacts.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper notes the need for pipelines to attribute model outputs and quantify originality when building on large internet corpora.

human_samples.population: Creators/channels whose public sketch-comedy videos were used as corpus/reference material: Foil Arms & Hog, Key & Peele, SNL, Studio C, and Viva La Dirt League.
human_samples.recruitment: Public YouTube channel corpus; the paper says videos not meeting length and format criteria were excluded, but does not specify the criteria.
human_samples.observations: 4,940 YouTube data points collected; 30 data points from each of 5 channels and each of 3 engagement tiers, yielding 450 tiered data points.
human_samples.detail: Human-produced YouTube sketch content supplied the engagement-scored corpus, critic-alignment examples, validation/test items, and middle-tier reference videos.

human_samples.population: Organic YouTube viewers of the five sketch-comedy channels.
human_samples.recruitment: Not recruited by the study; engagement inferred from platform view counts.
human_samples.observations: View counts for the 4,940 collected YouTube data points were normalized by video age using per-channel logistic growth models and used as engagement signals.
human_samples.detail: The paper treats normalized projected carrying capacity from view-count trajectories as a proxy for audience engagement/humor quality.

human_samples.population: Participants across the US, Europe, and Asia.
human_samples.observations: 22 responses per method and 110 responses total; five methods; each method represented by four videos; each participant viewed two videos per method and rated all five methods.
human_samples.detail: Blind randomized human evaluation of COMIC, VGoT, MovieAgent, Veo 3.1, and Sora 2 on 7-point Likert scales for funniness, re-watch/watch-more intent, comparison to human comedy, script quality, narrative quality, visual realism, and visual consistency.

human_samples.observations: Paired A/B preferences between COMIC and a critic-free baseline across funniness, script quality, narrative quality, visual realism, and visual consistency; neutral responses were excluded; the number of participants or comparisons is not reported.
human_samples.detail: Figure 10 reports non-neutral preference percentages: Script 57% vs 43%, Narrative 71% vs 29%, Realism 88% vs 12%, Consistency 100% for COMIC, and Funniness 62% vs 38%.

judges.detail: Human raters in the blind randomized baseline comparison determined Table 3 mean Likert scores for the five video-generation methods.
Human raters in the paired A/B ablation determined preferences between full COMIC and the critic-free baseline; the paper does not state whether this ablation was blinded.

judges.name: YouTube view counts
judges.detail: View counts, normalized by a per-channel logistic growth model and projected carrying capacity, supplied engagement-tier labels used to select and validate LLM critics.

judges.name: Claude 3.5 Haiku language-only critics
judges.detail: Language-only LLM critics performed pairwise script comparisons for critic alignment, script tournaments, and script-quality metrics; selected critics included Mean Critic, Single Best, and Task-Wise Best strategies.

judges.name: Gemini 3 Flash Preview multimodal critics
judges.detail: Multimodal/video critics evaluated rendered shots/videos, refined scene specifications, and performed pairwise video comparisons for automated evaluation.

judges.name: Claude 3.5 Opus meta-critic / script inspection / scene direction
judges.detail: Claude 3.5 Opus generated critic instructions, performed script inspection, and generated final scene directions; the supplement also says final merged-island evaluation used a specialized critic committee tailored to an academic audience.

judges.name: Image critic for best-of-batch selection
judges.detail: The supplement states that an image critic performed best-of-batch selection to improve image quality and consistency, but does not identify the critic model or batch size.

judges.name: Win Rate, Inter-Diversity, Intra-Diversity
judges.detail: These metrics aggregate pairwise evaluator probabilities from LLM critics for script/video quality and diversity analyses.

generation_selection.n: critic pool size of 10 in practice
generation_selection.detail: A meta-critic generated diverse critic personas; critics were retained by validation accuracy against YouTube engagement-tier comparison tasks. Task-Wise Best selected the highest-accuracy critic per channel and comparison task.

generation_selection.scope: COMIC script generation and script selection for production
generation_selection.n: base configuration uses K=3 islands and |S_k|=3 scripts per island; results are reported from the 4th generation unless otherwise noted; top four scripts selected for production
generation_selection.detail: Scripts evolved through round-robin pairwise LLM-critic tournaments; losing scripts were revised. After island iterations, scripts were inspected and ranked in a merged league. The supplement is not fully consistent about whether final selection used the single best critic from C_task or a specialized academic-audience critic committee.

generation_selection.scope: COMIC video rendering for selected scripts
generation_selection.n: base configuration uses D=2 scene directions and |C_render|=1 rendering critic; small/base/large use D=1/2/4 and |C_render|=0/1/2 respectively
generation_selection.detail: For each script, multiple scene directions were rendered; each shot kept a refinement history and was selected by a single-elimination tournament, followed by a final single-elimination tournament among complete video realizations. The paper also reports image best-of-batch selection, but not its n.

generation_selection.scope: COMIC videos in the human baseline comparison
generation_selection.n: four COMIC videos, generated from the top-performing scripts selected automatically by the pipeline
generation_selection.detail: The supplement states these four COMIC videos were selected automatically without manual curation.

generation_selection.scope: Baseline method videos in the human baseline comparison: VGoT, MovieAgent, Veo 3.1, and Sora 2
generation_selection.n: each baseline method was run four times independently to obtain four videos
generation_selection.detail: The paper does not report best-of-N selection or manual curation for baseline outputs.
The A/B study compares full COMIC with a critic-free baseline, but the paper does not report the number of generated ablation candidates or how ablation videos were selected.

model_provenance.name: COMIC composite system
model_provenance.detail: Uses API-budgeted language/multimodal calls plus local H200 image/video/voice generation; base configuration reported unless otherwise noted.

model_provenance.name: Claude
model_provenance.detail: Used for concept and script generation; no API snapshot, inference date, or decoding settings reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude
model_provenance.detail: Used for language-only critics; no API snapshot, inference date, or decoding settings reported.

model_provenance.name: Gemini
model_provenance.detail: Used for multimodal critics; no API snapshot, inference date, or decoding settings reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude
model_provenance.detail: Used for script inspection, scene direction generation, and meta-critic instruction generation; no API snapshot, inference date, or decoding settings reported.

model_provenance.name: FLUX.2 [dev] + TAG
model_provenance.detail: Used for character image synthesis and visual clarity enhancement; paper states image/video/voice generation was performed on an H200.

model_provenance.name: ElevenLabs
model_provenance.detail: Used for voice synthesis; exact model/version and access mode are not specified.

model_provenance.name: Chatterbox-TTS
model_provenance.detail: Used for voice cloning; paper states image/video/voice generation was performed on an H200, but no exact checkpoint is reported.

model_provenance.name: Wan
model_provenance.detail: Used for video rendering; paper states image/video/voice generation was performed on an H200.

model_provenance.name: Veo
model_provenance.detail: Black-box frontier text-to-video baseline; no inference date, access path, prompts, or sampling settings reported.

model_provenance.name: Sora
model_provenance.detail: Black-box frontier text-to-video baseline; no inference date, access path, prompts, or sampling settings reported.

model_provenance.name: VideoGen-of-Thought
model_provenance.detail: Agentic video-production baseline cited as VGoT; implementation/version and underlying model settings are not reported.

model_provenance.name: MovieAgent
model_provenance.detail: Agentic video-production baseline; implementation/version and underlying model settings are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Writing-stage cost is stated as O(G · K · |S_k|^2 · |C_k|) LLM-critic evaluations across G generations; a globally pooled tournament would be O(G · K^2 · |S_k|^2 · |C_k|).
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Small: K=2, |S_k|=2, |C_k|=2, D=1, |C_render|=0. Base: K=3, |S_k|=3, |C_k|=3, D=2, |C_render|=1. Large: K=4, |S_k|=4, |C_k|=4, D=4, |C_render|=2.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Diffusion rendering uses an additional condition-agnostic guidance term/TAG; no temperature, seed, or decoding parameters are reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Base configuration takes approximately one day on a single GPU / single H200 GPU with an API budget around $5.
inference_budgets.detail: Unless otherwise noted, experiments use the base configuration and report results from the 4th generation.

inference_budgets.scope: COMIC rendering stage per selected script
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: The supplement states O(D · N · |C_render|) render calls per script, shot-level tournament selection O(D · N · |C_render|^2) comparisons, and final video-level tournament O(D · |C_render|) comparisons; it then states the dominant rendering-stage term as O(D · N · |C_render|^2) render calls per script.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Each script receives D scene directions; each shot has a history {v_i^(0), ..., v_i^(|C_render|)}; a final tournament selects among D complete videos.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Render uses image, voice, and video generation with audio-visual-text conditions plus condition-agnostic guidance/TAG; exact diffusion sampling settings are not reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Image, video, and voice generation are reported as performed on an H200.
inference_budgets.detail: Base rendering uses D=2 and |C_render|=1; large uses D=4 and |C_render|=2.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Candidate critic pool size is 10 in practice; in-context sample counts tested were 0, 15, and 45; 30 data points per channel per tier produced 450 tiered data points split into S_in-context, S_val, and S_test.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports validation/test accuracies but not the number of LLM calls, prompts in full, token limits, or decoding settings for critic generation/evaluation.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Baseline methods were each run four times independently; COMIC generated videos from top-performing scripts selected automatically by its pipeline.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Four videos per method in the human evaluation; each participant viewed two videos per method.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper does not report prompts, seeds, sample lengths for each method, or whether frontier-model calls used any hidden retries.

inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Performed as part of image generation on H200 according to the supplement’s statement about image/video/voice generation.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper says an image critic performs best-of-batch selection, but the batch size, critic identity, and selection prompt are not reported.

human_baselines.scope: Human-evaluation comparison to average human sketch comedy
human_baselines.description: Participants rated each generated video on a 7-point item asking how funny it was compared to the average human-made sketch comedy they had ever seen.
human_baselines.detail: No actual human-made videos were shown or rated alongside model outputs for this item; the baseline is participants’ memory/impression of human comedy.

human_baselines.scope: Automated comparison against middle-tier YouTube sketch videos
human_baselines.description: Selected video critics compared generated videos against middle-tier test videos from the YouTube sketch corpus representing median sketch comedies.
human_baselines.detail: Human-produced and model-generated videos were compared by the same LLM-critic metric, but they were not generated under matched prompts/assets and were not directly judged by human raters in the same paired task.

human_baselines.scope: Matched human creator baseline from the same inputs
human_baselines.detail: The paper does not report human writers or filmmakers generating scripts/videos from the same character/background inputs under matched conditions.

mitigations: The 450 engagement-tier items were split into in-context, validation, and held-out test partitions for critic selection/evaluation.
Main video-quality claims are partly supported by a blind randomized human evaluation of newly generated videos.
The supplement states the four COMIC human-evaluation videos were selected automatically without manual curation.
contamination_risk.basis: The critic-alignment benchmark and automated video references are drawn from public YouTube sketch videos and public engagement signals, and the paper uses later proprietary/black-box LLMs and video models without reporting model snapshots, data cutoffs, collection dates, or any decontamination/memorization test. Public held-out splits reduce within-study overfitting but do not rule out pretraining exposure. Human evaluation of new generated outputs lowers the risk for some generation-quality claims, so the overall assessment is moderate rather than high.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Critic-alignment results in Tables 1–2, automated video ranking in Table 4, script/video selection within COMIC, generation/scale/island ablations, and automated win-rate/diversity claims depend on LLM critics or metrics derived from LLM pairwise judgments.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: The baseline comparison includes blind human ratings with 22 responses per method, and the no-critics ablation includes a human A/B study with neutral responses excluded; the paper also notes the automated video ranking aligns with Table 3 human results.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLM judges are not the only evidence source because Table 3 and the A/B ablation use human raters, but important methodological and performance claims are determined by LLM critic selection, LLM pairwise judging, and LLM-selected generation outputs.

reporting_gaps: Exact human participant count for the baseline evaluation is not reported; only responses per method and total responses are reported.
Recruitment method, compensation, demographic details, and participant language proficiency for human evaluations are not reported beyond geography for the baseline comparison.
A/B ablation participant count, number of paired comparisons, whether raters overlapped with the baseline study, geography, recruitment, and blinding are not reported.
Counts of neutral A/B responses excluded from Figure 10 are not reported.
Exact split sizes for S_in-context, S_val, and S_test are not reported, despite the 450 total tiered data points being reported.
YouTube data collection dates, publication-date ranges, and the exact length/format exclusion criteria are not reported.
No decontamination or memorization analysis is reported for public YouTube scripts/videos or engagement data used with LLM critics and generators.
Exact API snapshots, inference dates, and access paths are not reported for Claude, Gemini, Sora, Veo, VGoT, MovieAgent, or other model calls.
Prompts, token budgets, temperature/top-p/seed settings, retry policies, and decoding parameters are not reported for LLM agents or baselines.
Baseline-generation details for VGoT, MovieAgent, Veo 3.1, and Sora 2 are sparse: prompts, input assets, output durations, and any failed generations or retries are not specified.
The number of generated videos and pairwise comparisons used in the automated video evaluation of Table 4 is not reported.
The image critic used for best-of-batch selection is not identified, and its batch size and selection procedure are not reported.

The supplement gives inconsistent descriptions of final cross-island script selection: one section says the best validation critic from C_task is used, while another says a specialized academic-audience critic committee is used.
No confidence intervals, standard errors, significance tests, or inter-rater reliability statistics are reported for the human evaluation means or A/B percentages.
The paper reports approximate base compute/cost but not exact API call counts, per-component costs, or compute for small and large configurations.
